ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for ban mian or scallion oil noodles. FOODON_00002473 (noodle dish) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for ban mian or scallion oil noodles. A00LC (Pasta and noodles, cooked) is the closest defensible parent category.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 400 g, representing a single bowl of dry scallion oil ban mian with scallops, carrots, leafy greens, and corn as typically served in a Singaporean noodle restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Ban Mian (dry, with toppings) — estimated from HPB Singapore Foodo component data: cooked flat wheat noodles, scallion oil, dried scallops, vegetables
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) — Foodo / Singapore Food Composition Database; supplemented by USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for scallion oil and vegetable components
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single HPB Foodo entry exists for dry scallion oil ban mian with scallops. Values are estimated by weighted component assembly: approximately 60% cooked flat wheat noodles (HPB: ~145 kcal/100g), 15% scallion oil dressing (USDA SR Legacy: ~450 kcal/100g for scallion-infused oil), 10% dried/rehydrated scallops (USDA FoodData Central: ~111 kcal/100g), 15% mixed vegetables — carrots, leafy greens, corn (HPB average: ~40 kcal/100g). Weighted blend yields approximately 165 kcal/100g. All macros derived from this component-weighted approach. Sodium estimated from typical Singaporean noodle dish seasoning (soy sauce, oyster sauce) per HPB reference data.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of wheat noodles and mixed noodle dishes — analogous estimate based on flat wheat noodles (GI ~47) from Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC (2008) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values, Diabetes Care 31(12):2281–2283
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; peer-reviewed journal: Diabetes Care
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for dry scallion oil ban mian. GI of 47 is adopted from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for flat wheat noodles (Chinese-style, boiled), which is the primary carbohydrate component. The scallion oil dressing and protein from scallops are expected to further attenuate postprandial glucose response, making GI 47 a conservative upper-bound estimate. Glycemic load calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (47 × 82.0) / 100 = 38.5, rounded to 38.6 for a 400 g serving providing approximately 82 g available carbohydrate.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content approximately 420 mg per 100 g; a 400 g serving delivers approximately 1680 mg sodium, representing 73% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2300 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce and seasoning when ordering, and limit portion size to manage sodium intake within the 2300 mg/day threshold recommended by the Singapore HPB.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load of approximately 38.6 per standard 400 g serving despite a low GI of 47, due to the substantial carbohydrate content from flat wheat noodles.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider a reduced noodle portion (200–250 g serving) to lower glycemic load, and pair with additional non-starchy vegetables and protein from scallops to blunt postprandial glucose excursion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Scallion oil contributes moderate total fat (6.8 g/100g) with a favourable unsaturated-to-saturated ratio; however, sodium load and cholesterol from scallops warrant attention.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should monitor overall sodium intake from this dish and favour smaller portions; the predominantly unsaturated fat profile from scallion oil is relatively heart-friendly compared to lard-based noodle dishes.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dry Scallion Oil Ban Mian is a Singaporean Chinese noodle dish featuring hand-pulled or machine-cut flat wheat noodles (ban mian) tossed in fragrant scallion-infused oil rather than served in a soup broth. This 'dry' preparation style allows the noodles to absorb the aromatic scallion oil, yielding a glossy, savoury coating with a rich umami depth. The dish is topped with dried or rehydrated scallops, fresh julienned carrots, leafy greens such as spinach or chye sim, and sweet corn kernels, providing textural contrast and nutritional variety. Ban mian noodles have a slightly chewy, dense texture characteristic of thick flat wheat noodles. Scallion oil is prepared by slowly infusing neutral oil with fried scallions until deeply golden, producing a condiment central to the dish's flavour identity. Nutritionally, the dish provides moderate energy (~165 kcal/100g), a meaningful protein contribution from scallops (~7.2 g/100g), and a low-to-moderate glycemic index of approximately 47 due to the dense wheat noodle matrix. Sodium content is elevated (~420 mg/100g) from seasoning sauces, making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The vegetable components contribute dietary fibre, vitamin A precursors from carrots, and vitamin C. This dish is suitable for general consumption but requires portion awareness for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to the substantial carbohydrate load from noodles in a full serving.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for dry scallion oil ban mian with scallops. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component assembly using HPB Singapore Foodo and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for individual components (cooked flat wheat noodles, scallion oil, scallops, mixed vegetables). GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for flat wheat noodles. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-weighted estimates; no single HPB Foodo or USDA entry covers this exact dish. A direct laboratory analysis or HPB-verified composite entry would upgrade confidence to high. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the component source databases for this dish type. 3. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the component source databases for this dish type. 4. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the component source databases for this dish type. 5. Trans fat is reported as 0.0; this is confirmed zero by formulation — scallion oil and wheat noodles do not contain industrially produced trans fats, and no trans fat is reported in the USDA SR Legacy entries for the component ingredients. 6. GI of 47 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for flat wheat noodles (Chinese-style, boiled); no direct GI measurement for this specific dish exists. 7. Sodium estimate of 420 mg/100g reflects typical Singaporean noodle dish seasoning (soy sauce, oyster sauce) and may vary significantly by restaurant preparation. 8. The dish is not certified halal; scallops are generally permissible but the scallion oil preparation and seasoning sauces require halal certification verification. 9. Serving size of 400 g is an estimate for a standard restaurant bowl; actual serving weights may vary between 350–500 g.
